tossups.question: In one of this author’s novels, a man is transfixed by the heroine’s bracelet falling down her arm and being replaced, and by the way that she used her fingers as sugar tongs. In another of this author’s works, Mr. Benson reads from Revelation 7 because he is too distraught to deliver a sermon when he remembers first meeting the dead heroine at Llan-dhu [hlan-du] with her wealthy lover Mr. Bellingham whom she had died nursing. In a letter, Charles Dickens described this writer as ‘dear Scheherazade’ and one of their works serialized in Household Works tells of Margaret Hale’s move from her hometown ‘like a village in a poem’, Helstone to the Darkshire town of Milton. For 10 points, name this Victorian novelist of Ruth, and North and South.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one of this author's novels, a man is transfixed by the heroine's bracelet falling down her arm and being replaced, and by the way that she used her fingers as sugar tongs. In another of this author's works, Mr. Benson reads from Revelation 7 because he is too distraught to deliver a sermon when he remembers first meeting the dead heroine at Llan-dhu [hlan-du] with her wealthy lover Mr. Bellingham whom she had died nursing. In a letter, Charles Dickens described this writer as 'dear Scheherazade' and one of their works serialized in Household Works tells of Margaret Hale's move from her hometown 'like a village in a poem', Helstone to the Darkshire town of Milton. For 10 points, name this Victorian novelist of Ruth, and North and South.

tossups.question: In one of this author’s stories, a rural worker who leads a second life as a poacher has his attempts at redemption repeatedly foiled by the thought of his enemy Dick Jackson. Job Legh collects insects in a novel by this author, in which the main story follows the reconciliation between a mill-owning and a mill-working family. As well as Mary Barton, this author wrote a novel in which Margaret Hale falls in love with Mr Thornton and sheds some of her assumptions about the North. For 10 points, identify this author of Cranford and North and South.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one of this author's stories, a rural worker who leads a second life as a poacher has his attempts at redemption repeatedly foiled by the thought of his enemy Dick Jackson. Job Legh collects insects in a novel by this author, in which the main story follows the reconciliation between a mill-owning and a mill-working family. As well as Mary Barton, this author wrote a novel in which Margaret Hale falls in love with Mr Thornton and sheds some of her assumptions about the North. For 10 points, identify this author of Cranford and North and South.

tossups.question: In a novel by this author, the entire town delights in hearing a story about a cat who swallowed a piece of lace and how the lace was later cleaned after being excreted, and Betsy Barker attempts to save a cow that had fallen into a lime‐pit by dressing it in flannel. This author wrote a novel in which the title character becomes a nurse to typhoid patients and again re‐encounters the MP Donne, who is actually the nefarious seducer (*) Bellingham. Thornton studies classics and decides to treat his workers beter in another novel by this writer, in which the Hale family moves to Milton. This author of Ruth and Wives and Daughters created Miss Matty, who must sell tea after losing her money in a bank collapse in a novel narrated by Mary Smith. For 10 points, name this author of North and South and Cranford.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a novel by this author, the entire town delights in hearing a story about a cat who swallowed a piece of lace and how the lace was later cleaned after being excreted, and Betsy Barker attempts to save a cow that had fallen into a lime-pit by dressing it in flannel. This author wrote a novel in which the title character becomes a nurse to typhoid patients and again re-encounters the MP Donne, who is actually the nefarious seducer (*) Bellingham. Thornton studies classics and decides to treat his workers beter in another novel by this writer, in which the Hale family moves to Milton. This author of Ruth and Wives and Daughters created Miss Matty, who must sell tea after losing her money in a bank collapse in a novel narrated by Mary Smith. For 10 points, name this author of North and South and Cranford.

bonuses.leadin: This magazine published the story "A House to Let," whose four collaborating authors included Wilkie Collins and Adelaide Anne Proctor. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this weekly magazine published in the 1850s, before it was directly replaced by its successor All the Year Round.
This editor of Household Words and writer of Hard Times was one of the co-authors of "A House to Let."
The final author of "A House to Let" was this author of a biography of Charlotte Bronte, who serialized her novels Cranford and North and South in Household Words.
answers_sanitized: Elizabeth Gaskell [Elizabeth Cleghorn Gaskell or Elizabeth Cleghorn Stevenson]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This magazine published the story "A House to Let," whose four collaborating authors included Wilkie Collins and Adelaide Anne Proctor. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this weekly magazine published in the 1850s, before it was directly replaced by its successor All the Year Round.
This editor of Household Words and writer of Hard Times was one of the co-authors of "A House to Let."
The final author of "A House to Let" was this author of a biography of Charlotte Bronte, who serialized her novels Cranford and North and South in Household Words.

bonuses.leadin: Note to moderator: Please read the answerline for the first part carefully. Right after this book’s publication, its author fled to Rome with their daughters Marianne and Meta, and proceeded to perhaps fall in love with Charles Eliot Norton. For 10 points each:
parts: Name or describe this book, whose author blithely noted that they had “three people [whom they wanted] to libel” in it. This book blames Lydia Robinson for the death of its subject’s brother Branwell.
While in Rome, Gaskell stayed at the house of this sculptor, the inspiration for Kenyon in Hawthorne’s The Marble Faun. Henry James wrote a biography titled [this sculptor] And His Friends.

Nell Stevens’s fictionalization of Gaskell’s time in Rome includes a chapter describing Harriet Hosmer’s casting of the Clasped Hands of these two people. One of these two people was the addressee of the other’s line, “Yes, call me by my pet-name!”
answers: The Life of Charlotte Brontë [accept Elizabeth Gaskell’s biography of Charlotte Brontë or equivalent descriptions; prompt on answers that describe a biography of Charlotte Brontë without naming its author by asking “By whom?”; prompt on answers that describe an Elizabeth Gaskell biography of a Brontë sister but that do not indicate which one by asking “which Brontë?”; prompt on answers that describe an Elizabeth Gaskell book on Charlotte Brontë, but that do not indicate what genre of book]
William Wetmore Story [accept William Wetmore Story and His Friends] (Story’s sculpture Cleopatra is attributed to Kenyon in The Marble Faun.)
Robert Browning AND Elizabeth Barrett Browning [accept the Brownings; accept Elizabeth Barrett in place of “Elizabeth Barrett Browning”] (Nell Stevens wrote Mrs. Gaskell and Me.)

packet.name: 08 - Bollinger et al, Passement et al

answers_sanitized: The Life of Charlotte Bronte [accept Elizabeth Gaskell's biography of Charlotte Bronte or equivalent descriptions; prompt on answers that describe a biography of Charlotte Bronte without naming its author by asking "By whom?"; prompt on answers that describe an Elizabeth Gaskell biography of a Bronte sister but that do not indicate which one by asking "which Bronte?"; prompt on answers that describe an Elizabeth Gaskell book on Charlotte Bronte, but that do not indicate what genre of book]
William Wetmore Story [accept William Wetmore Story and His Friends] (Story's sculpture Cleopatra is attributed to Kenyon in The Marble Faun.)
Robert Browning AND Elizabeth Barrett Browning [accept the Brownings; accept Elizabeth Barrett in place of "Elizabeth Barrett Browning"] (Nell Stevens wrote Mrs. Gaskell and Me.)

bonuses.leadin: In this story, the Cambridge “ontography” professor Parkins takes a golfing holiday to Burnstow, where he finds a bronze object bearing the legends “FUR FLA FLE BIS” and “QUIS EST ISTE QUI UENIT” in the ruins of a Templar preceptory. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this story, titled for a Robert Burns poem, which ends after Parkins is narrowly saved from a being wrapped in bedclothes.

M.R. James's “Oh, Whistle and I'll Come to You, My Lad” appears in a collection titled for this genre of story narrated by an antiquary. The full title of A Christmas Carol describes it as one of these stories “of Christmas.”

This author first made her name with ghost stories like “The Old Nurse’s Story,” which was commissioned for the magazine Household Words. She chose to omit salacious details from her biography of Charlotte Brontë.
answers: “Oh, Whistle and I'll Come to You, My Lad”
ghost story/stories/fiction/etc. [accept Ghost Stories of an Antiquary; prompt on supernatural or horror stories, or similar less specific answers]
Elizabeth Gaskell [or Elizabeth Cleghorn Stevenson; accept Mrs Gaskell from weird Brits]
answers_sanitized: "Oh, Whistle and I'll Come to You, My Lad"
ghost story/stories/fiction/etc. [accept Ghost Stories of an Antiquary; prompt on supernatural or horror stories, or similar less specific answers]
Elizabeth Gaskell [or Elizabeth Cleghorn Stevenson; accept Mrs Gaskell from weird Brits]

bonuses.leadin: The first biography of this author omitted her obsession with the married Constantin Héger, who may have been the basis for M. Paul Emanuel in Villette. For 10 points each:
parts: That biography draws on the extensive correspondences between Ellen Nussey and what author of Jane Eyre?
The author of The Life of Charlotte Brontë also wrote this novel in which John Thornton contends with strikes at his cotton mill and eventually marries Margaret Hale.
Elizabeth Gaskell often feuded with this author, who published North and South in his magazine Household Words. This author also wrote a novel about the case Jarndyce and Jarndyce.
answers: Charles Dickens [or Charles John Huffam Dickens] (The novel is Bleak House.)

Charles Dickens [or Charles John Huffam Dickens] (The novel is Bleak House.)

bonuses.leadin: A woman who serves this foodstuff at Weydon-Priors fair adds alcohol to it if customers wink at her and pay her extra. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this foodstuff. In The Mayor of Casterbridge, Michael Henchard consumes too much of this foodstuff, which has been spiked with rum, causing him to become belligerently drunk and sell his wife and child.
Alice is told by a gnat that the snap-dragon-fly lives only on mince pies and frumenty in this sequel to Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland.

Margaret Dawson learns to make frumenty at Hanbury Court in this author’s novel My Lady Ludlow. This author wrote a novel in which Margaret Hale moves from the village of Helstone to the industrial town of Milton.
answers: frumenty [or furmity; or fromity; or fermenty; prompt on porridge; prompt on mash]
Through the Looking-Glass, and What Alice Found There [or Alice Through the Looking-Glass]
Elizabeth Gaskell [or Elizabeth Cleghorn Stevenson] (The latter novel is North and South.)
answers_sanitized: frumenty [or furmity; or fromity; or fermenty; prompt on porridge; prompt on mash]
Through the Looking-Glass, and What Alice Found There [or Alice Through the Looking-Glass]
Elizabeth Gaskell [or Elizabeth Cleghorn Stevenson] (The latter novel is North and South.)

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, name some works of Victorian literature which comment on temperance:
parts: In this novel, Helen Graham’s brother Mr Lawrence notes that “with some persons, temperance is almost impossible”. Helen’s husband Arthur Huntingdon dies from alcoholism, allowing her to marry Gilbert Markham.

Leonards dies after a fall exacerbates an injury caused by his intemperance and Mr Thornton praises the virtues of sober conduct to Margaret Hale in North and South, a novel by this author of Cranford.

A working class radical named Devilsdust in this novel by the author of Coningsby states, “we shall never get our rights till we leave off consuming exciseable articles; and the best thing to begin with is liquors”.
answers: Sibyl, or The Two Nations [accept The Two Nations]
answers_sanitized: Sibyl, or The Two Nations [accept The Two Nations]

bonuses.leadin: This essay is particularly critical of “mind-and-millinery” novels. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this George Eliot essay that attacks books with beautiful, impeccable, well-read female protagonists as being detrimental to the education of women in the Victorian era.
In the essay, Eliot does praise a select few female novelists including this author of Mary Barton and North and South .

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about “Condition of England” novels, a body of fiction investigating social issues in the wake of the Industrial Revolution in Britain, for 10 points each.
parts: The term derives from the “Condition of England” question raised by this author in his essay “Chartism.” In his only novel, an unnamed Editor preoccupied with contemporary social issues struggles to review a philosophical study of clothes authored by Diogenes Teufelsdröckh.

The preface to this novel expresses the author’s sympathy with those “doomed to struggle through their lives in strange alternations between work and want.” Its title character loves Jem Wilson, who had saved his father and a worker from a fire at the Carson Mill.

This author wrote that the gulf between the rich and poor in England had grown so wide that its citizens live in “two nations,” a phrase which subtitles his novel Sybil. He is better-known for being Britain’s only Jewish prime minister.
answers_sanitized: Thomas Carlyle [the novel is Sartor Resartus]

bonuses.leadin: The author of this novel considered calling it Death and Variations, because each character dies in a manner consistent with their personality. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel in which Margaret Hale moves from rural Helstone to the impoverished town of Darkshire. She eventually marries John Thornton, who shuts down his cotton mill due to a strike.
North and South was written by this Victorian novelist of Wives and Daughters and Cranford.

Gaskell wrote about Jem Wilson’s love for a title character with this first name and last name Barton. The first part of Cranford is narrated by a title character with this first name, the same as the author of Frankenstein.

bonuses.leadin: Name these Victorian authors of novels about fallen women, for 10 points each.
parts: Ex-prostitute Mercy Merrick adopts the identity of the Franco-Prussian War casualty Grace Roseberry in The New Mag- 7 dalen, an out-of-print novel by this author who’s better known for The Woman in White.

An orphan sweatshop girl is swept into an afair with the rakish aristocrat Henry Bellingham in this author’s depressing book Ruth, and the title character’s sister Esther becomes a streetwalker in her novel Mary Barton.

This author wrote a novel in which the kitchen-maid Esther is impregnated by the footman William Latch, but resolves to raise the child despite facing hardships such as her family moving to Australia.

packet.name: Round 13 - Northwestern, William and Mary, and Stanford

answers_sanitized: George Moore [the book is Esther Waters]

bonuses.leadin: This novel is set in the industrial city of Milton, which is modeled after Manchester. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel whose protagonist, Margaret Hale, meets the Union leader Nicholas Higgins and eventually marries John Thornton, the owner of a cotton mill.
North and South was written by this woman who also wrote a biography of Charlotte Brontë. Her novels include Mary Barton and Cranford.
Many of Gaskell's works were published in Household Words, a journal published by this creator of Ebenezer Scooge.

parts_sanitized: Name this novel whose protagonist, Margaret Hale, meets the Union leader Nicholas Higgins and eventually marries John Thornton, the owner of a cotton mill.
North and South was written by this woman who also wrote a biography of Charlotte Bronte. Her novels include Mary Barton and Cranford.
Many of Gaskell's works were published in Household Words, a journal published by this creator of Ebenezer Scooge.

bonuses.leadin: At a strike in this novel, the main female character steps out of a barricaded house to quell the rioters, while Higgins goes to work to support the family of his friend who killed himself. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel in which Mr. Thornton eventually runs his mill in cooperation with his workers, which allows him to win the hand of Margaret Hale.
North and South was written by this author who created the spinster sisters Miss Matty and Miss Deborah in Cranford and also penned Mary Barton.

Gaskell is also known for a biographical work about this other author who wrote about a woman who dies with the name of her beloved Walter on her lips in her poem “Apostasy.” She also penned the novel Villette.
